claims.claim: The Sultan of Johor is concurrently Yang di-Pertuan Agong.
claims.source: Portal Kesultanan Johor, Kerabat Damping, accessed 2026-05-10, https://royal.johor.my/kerabat-damping/ ; Istana Negara, Profil KDYMM Seri Paduka Baginda Yang di-Pertuan Agong Sultan Ibrahim, accessed 2026-05-10, https://www.istananegara.gov.my/v1/istana-negara/kdymm-spb-ydpa-xvii/profil-agong
claims.stale_check: Current-office claim checked against current official royal sources on 2026-05-10.

claims.claim: On 7 May 2026, the Johor State Assembly amended the Undang-undang Tubuh Negeri Johor 1895 to allow up to five appointed assemblymen, raising the assembly's maximum size from 56 to 61.
claims.source: No official 7 May 2026 bill text, gazette, or accessible Hansard located within reasonable effort. Official Johor Penyata Rasmi page, accessed 2026-05-10, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/pr/ ; official Johor Rang Undang-Undang / Enakmen page, accessed 2026-05-10, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/rang-undang-undang-enakmen/ ; official Johor UUTNJ page confirms the title Undang-Undang Tubuh Negeri Johor 1895, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/uutnj/
claims.correction: Do not publish this as settled fact until the official 2026 bill, gazette, or Hansard is obtained.
claims.stale_check: A real-world change after the official Johor answer of 11 September 2025 is possible, but the exact 7 May 2026 claim still lacks a primary 2026 record.

claims.claim: The vote was 40 in favour and 16 against.
claims.source: No official 7 May 2026 division record or accessible Hansard located within reasonable effort. Official Johor Penyata Rasmi page, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/pr/
claims.correction: This needs an official division record or Hansard before publication.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue identified; the problem is lack of primary verification.

claims.claim: The 7 May 2026 vote matched the 2022 state-election seat count for Barisan Nasional and the rest.
claims.source: The 2022 seat arithmetic is supported by SPR's official results portal for PRU DUN Johor Ke-15, https://mysprsemak.spr.gov.my/semakan/keputusan/pru-dun, and Bernama's final tally quoting SPR on 2022-03-13, https://www.bernama.com/bm/news.php?id=2061330. The 7 May 2026 vote itself was not primary-verified.
claims.correction: Split the claim: the 2022 BN/non-BN arithmetic is supportable; the claimed 2026 vote match is not yet primary-verified.

claims.claim: The official 2022 Johor result was BN 40, PH 11, PN 3, PKR 1, and MUDA 1.
claims.source: Suruhanjaya Pilihan Raya Malaysia official results portal for PRU DUN Johor Ke-15, accessed 2026-05-10, https://mysprsemak.spr.gov.my/semakan/keputusan/pru-dun ; Bernama final tally quoting SPR, 2022-03-13, https://www.bernama.com/bm/news.php?id=2061330
claims.stale_check: Historical election result; no stale issue identified.

claims.claim: Johor Expanded Its Assembly By Exactly The Seats BN Won In 2022.
claims.source: SPR official results portal for PRU DUN Johor Ke-15, https://mysprsemak.spr.gov.my/semakan/keputusan/pru-dun ; Bernama final tally quoting SPR, 2022-03-13, https://www.bernama.com/bm/news.php?id=2061330
claims.correction: Five proposed extra seats are not equal to the 40 seats BN won in 2022. If the intended point is that a 40-16 vote would mirror the BN/non-BN split, say that instead, subject to primary verification of the vote.
claims.stale_check: Not a stale issue; it is a mathematical error in the headline.

claims.claim: Five new members will be appointed, not elected.
claims.source: No official 7 May 2026 Johor bill text, gazette, or accessible Hansard located within reasonable effort.
claims.correction: Requires the official Johor amendment text.
claims.stale_check: Possible recent change after 11 September 2025; unverified as of 2026-05-10.

claims.claim: BN cleared the two-thirds bar by exactly two votes.
claims.source: Official Johor answer dated 2025-09-11 states state constitutional amendments require support of not less than two-thirds of members, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf ; the alleged 40-16 vote was not primary-verified.
claims.correction: The arithmetic is conditionally correct only if the unverified 40-16 vote occurred and the denominator was all 56 members. Do not state it as fact without the official vote record.

claims.claim: The next state election is constitutionally due by March 2027.
claims.source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, section 9(3)-(4), official BHESS reprint as at 2020-10-15, https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf ; official Johor Speaker page states the first sitting of the 15th Assembly was on 2022-04-21, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/speaker/
claims.correction: Absent earlier dissolution, the assembly runs five years from its first sitting on 21 April 2022 and would dissolve in April 2027; the election must then be held within 60 days.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; contradicted by standing law and the official first-sitting date.

claims.claim: The Federal Constitution's Eighth Schedule says state assemblies have 'such number of elected members as State law provides.'
claims.source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, section 4(1), official BHESS reprint as at 2020-10-15, https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf

claims.claim: It does not, on its face, ban additional appointed members.
claims.source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, section 4(1) and Part II section 21(1), official BHESS reprint, https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf ; official Johor answer dated 2025-09-11, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf
claims.correction: The ordinary-state text in section 4(1) speaks only of elected members. Where the Schedule intends additional members, section 21(1) says so expressly. Johor's own official 2025 answer said appointed ADUN were viewed as inconsistent with the Federal Constitution and legally disputable.

claims.claim: DAP's Nga Kor Ming calls the amendment unconstitutional.
claims.source: No primary source for the attribution located within reasonable effort.
claims.correction: Use only if a direct statement, official transcript, or other primary record is obtained.

claims.claim: UMNO points out Pahang and Sabah did this first.
claims.source: No primary source for the attribution located within reasonable effort.
claims.correction: Use only if a direct statement or official record is obtained.

claims.claim: No Federal Court ruling has tested the question.
claims.source: No official Federal Court judgment text on this issue was located within reasonable effort, but an absence search cannot prove non-existence.
claims.correction: State only that no relevant Federal Court ruling was located in this review, not that none exists.

claims.claim: Pahang ran the same playbook in 2024.
claims.source: Pahang official government release, 2024-09-05, https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf
claims.correction: Pahang's five appointees were sworn in in 2024, but the enabling amendment was passed in November 2020. 'Same playbook' is editorial framing, not a primary-verifiable fact.

claims.claim: All five appointed reps were ruling-coalition party officials, not the technocrats the rationale promised.
claims.source: Pahang official member list, updated 2026-05-10, https://www.pahang.gov.my/index.php/pages/view/666?mid=47 ; Pahang official government release, 2024-09-05, https://www.pahang.gov.my/pahang/resources/PDF/Berita/2024/September/%285hb%29_LIMA_ADUN_LANTIKAN_SELESAI_ANGKAT_SUMPAH_HARI_INI.pdf
claims.correction: Official sources show all five appointees had party labels, but they do not establish that all five were 'party officials'. The official rationale referred to 'golongan berkaliber', better service, and being people-oriented rather than political; it did not promise 'technocrats'.

claims.claim: Pahang appointed an UMNO division chief, an MCA division chief, an Amanah state chairman, a PKR deputy state chairman, and a tahfiz-school founder.
claims.source: Pahang official government release confirms the five names, 2024-09-05; official Pahang member list confirms party labels. No primary source was located for the specific organisational titles in the claim.
claims.correction: Do not state the specific party offices unless each title is independently verified from primary sources.

claims.claim: None were non-aligned experts.
claims.source: Pahang official member list, updated 2026-05-10, https://www.pahang.gov.my/index.php/pages/view/666?mid=47
claims.correction: Official sources support that all five were party-affiliated. They do not establish whether any were experts, so the 'experts' part is unsupported.

claims.claim: Onn Hafiz ruled out an immediate snap poll on the same day the amendment passed.
claims.source: No primary source for the attribution located within reasonable effort.
claims.correction: Use only if a direct official statement, transcript, or press release is obtained.

claims.claim: Five appointments require seven seconders and a simple majority, a threshold BN clears alone.
claims.source: Pahang official government release confirms this process for Pahang; no official Johor amendment text was located.
claims.correction: Do not transfer the Pahang procedure to Johor unless the Johor amendment text is obtained.
claims.stale_check: Possible recent change after 11 September 2025; unverified as of 2026-05-10.

claims.claim: The Sultan must assent within 30 days, or the Bill becomes law without him.
claims.source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, section 11(2a)-(3), official BHESS reprint, https://bhess.jpm.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Perlembagaan-Persekutuan-Cetakan-Semula-2020-1.pdf
claims.correction: The 30-day assent rule and deemed-law rule are correct, but section 11(3) also says no law takes effect until it has been published.

claims.claim: Appointed reps participate and vote, but cannot become MB or join the State Executive Council.
claims.source: Pahang official government release confirms these rights and restrictions for Pahang; no official Johor amendment text was located.
claims.correction: State this only as Pahang precedent unless and until the Johor clauses are obtained.
claims.stale_check: Possible recent change after 11 September 2025; unverified as a Johor claim.

claims.claim: Four states now allow appointed assemblymen.
claims.source: Official sources verify Sabah, Pahang, and Terengganu mechanisms: Sabah Constitution Article 14(1)(c), https://sagc.sabah.gov.my/sites/default/files/law/Constitution%20of%20the%20State%20of%20Sabah%20.pdf ; Pahang official government release, 2024-09-05; Terengganu official constitution result, https://dun.terengganu.gov.my/files/LINK/undangtubuhterbaru.pdf. Johor was not primary-verified.
claims.correction: At primary-source standard, three states are verified in this review; Johor remains unverified pending official 2026 text.

claims.claim: Sabah (6 since Dec 2025).
claims.source: Sabah Constitution Article 14(1)(c), official Sabah AGC text, September 2025; original Sabah Constitution in Agreement Relating to Malaysia, https://sagc.sabah.gov.my/sites/default/files/law/AgreementRelatingToMalaysia.pdf ; official Sabah Chief Minister media release, 2025-12-11, https://cm.sabah.gov.my/siaran-media/4410
claims.correction: If referring to the current cohort, six appointed ADUN were sworn in on 11 December 2025. If referring to the legal power to appoint up to six members, that existed in the original 1963 Sabah Constitution, not only since December 2025.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; the issue is conflation of appointment date with legal-authority date.

claims.claim: Terengganu (up to 4 since 1999).
claims.source: Terengganu official constitution result supports a ceiling of four appointed members; official Terengganu Hansard confirms an appointed member under Fasal 27(2) in 2018, https://dun.terengganu.gov.my/files/LINK/2018m2p1d14.pdf. No primary source was located in this review proving the mechanism began in 1999.
claims.correction: Separate the verified 'up to four' rule from the unverified 'since 1999' date.

claims.claim: Pahang (5 since May 2024).
claims.source: Pahang official government release states the five appointees were sworn in on 2024-09-05 and that the enabling amendment was passed in November 2020.
claims.correction: The primary record found supports November 2020 for the enabling amendment and 5 September 2024 for the swearing-in, not 'since May 2024'.

claims.claim: Johor (up to 5, 7 May 2026).
claims.source: No official 7 May 2026 Johor bill text, gazette, or accessible Hansard located within reasonable effort.
claims.correction: Needs primary Johor text before publication.
claims.stale_check: Possible recent change after 11 September 2025; unverified as of 2026-05-10.

claims.claim: Pahang's 5/5 party-affiliated record is the only filled-out precedent.
claims.source: Pahang official member list; official Sabah Chief Minister media release recording six appointed ADUN on 2025-12-11; official Terengganu Hansard recording an appointed ADUN under Fasal 27(2) in 2018.
claims.correction: Pahang's five appointees are officially shown as party-affiliated, but it is not the only filled-out precedent. Sabah had six current appointees sworn in in December 2025, and Terengganu has official records of appointed members.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; contradicted by existing official records.

claims.claim: Onn Hafiz Ghazi was sworn in on 15 March 2022.
claims.source: Portal Kesultanan Johor, Raja, accessed 2026-05-10, https://royal.johor.my/raja/ ; Bernama, 2026-03-14, https://www.bernama.com/bm/news.php?id=2534309
claims.stale_check: Historical appointment date; no stale issue identified.

claims.claim: Onn Hafiz Ghazi sworn in 15 Mar 2022 - 5-year term sets March 2027 constitutional deadline.
claims.source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, section 9(3)-(4); official Johor Speaker page, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/speaker/
claims.correction: The assembly term runs from the first sitting, not the Menteri Besar's swearing-in. The official first sitting was 21 April 2022.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; this is a legal-anchor error.

claims.claim: Undang-Undang Tubuh Negeri Johor 1895.
claims.source: Official Dewan Negeri Johor UUTNJ page, accessed 2026-05-10, https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/uutnj/

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Johor Expanded Its Assembly By Exactly The Seats BN Won In 2022.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Five proposed extra seats are not equal to the 40 seats BN won in 2022. The headline overstates the arithmetic and is literally false.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: SPR official results portal; Bernama final tally quoting SPR, 2022-03-13

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: It does not, on its face, ban additional appointed members.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The ordinary-state text in Eighth Schedule section 4(1) speaks only of elected members, while the separate replacement provision in section 21(1) expressly mentions other members. Johor's own official 2025 answer said appointed ADUN were viewed as inconsistent with the Federal Constitution.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, sections 4(1) and 21(1); official Johor answer, 2025-09-11

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: All five appointed reps were ruling-coalition party officials - not the technocrats the rationale promised.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Official records show party labels, not that all five were party officials, and the official rationale did not promise technocrats.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Pahang official member list; Pahang official government release, 2024-09-05

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: On 7 May 2026, the Johor State Assembly amended the Undang-undang Tubuh Negeri Johor 1895 to allow up to five appointed assemblymen, raising the assembly's maximum size from 56 to 61.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No official Johor bill text, gazette, or accessible 7 May 2026 Hansard was located within reasonable effort, so the central mechanic remains unsupported at primary-source standard.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Official Johor Penyata Rasmi page; official Johor Rang Undang-Undang / Enakmen page

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The vote was 40 in favour and 16 against.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The analysis relies on secondary reporting; no official division record or accessible official Hansard was located.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The next state election is constitutionally due by March 2027.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The constitutional term runs from the assembly's first sitting, not the Menteri Besar's swearing-in or polling day. The first sitting was 21 April 2022, which points to April 2027 dissolution absent earlier dissolution.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Constitution, Eighth Schedule, section 9(3)-(4); official Johor Speaker page

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Sabah (6 since Dec 2025), Terengganu (up to 4 since 1999), Pahang (5 since May 2024), Johor (up to 5, 7 May 2026).
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This mixes legal-authority dates, appointment dates, and unverified enactment dates in one list.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Sabah Constitution Article 14(1)(c); Pahang official release; Terengganu official constitution result

omitted_facts: Johor's own official answer on 2025-09-11 said the state had no plan at that time to introduce appointed ADUN, cited Eighth Schedule section 4, and stated that the State Legal Adviser's Office viewed appointed ADUN as inconsistent with the Federal Constitution and legally disputable. Source: https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/SOALAN-LISAN-YANG-TIDAK-HABIS-DIJAWAB-PADA-11-SEPT-2025.pdf

The official Johor assembly site still described the chamber as one Speaker plus 56 elected members, and its current member list showed BN 40, DAP 10, PPBM 2, PAS 1, Amanah 1, PKR 1, and MUDA 1 as of access on 2026-05-10. Sources: https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/sejarah/ ; https://dewannegeri.johor.gov.my/sad/
The legal anchor for the next election is the assembly's first sitting, not the Menteri Besar's swearing-in. The official first sitting of the 15th Johor Assembly was 2022-04-21.

Pahang's own official explanation said the amendment was meant for persons of calibre, better service to the people, and being people-oriented rather than political; it also confirmed voting rights and the MB/EXCO exclusion.
Sabah's power to appoint up to six nominated members existed in the original 1963 Constitution, while the six current appointees were sworn in on 2025-12-11.
As of 2026-05-10, the official Johor Penyata Rasmi and bill pages did not expose the 7 May 2026 official Hansard or bill text needed to verify the central Johor claims.
source_assessment: The original analysis cites about 13 unique URLs, but the source base is media-heavy and weak on primary materials. It spans several commercial outlets and reports both DAP and UMNO positions, yet it omits the strongest available primary sources: official Johor assembly materials, the official Johor answer of 11 September 2025, official Pahang government documents, official Sabah and Terengganu constitutional texts, and official election or court materials. Institutional coverage is narrow, with little academic, NGO, business, or community input.
overall_assessment: The analysis contains several correct building blocks, but it is not publication-ready as a fully verified piece. Its central Johor-specific claims remain unsupported by an accessible primary 2026 source, its election-deadline claim is wrong, its constitutional framing is more permissive than the ordinary-state text and Johor's own prior official legal position support, and its state-comparison chronology mixes legal-authority dates with appointment dates. The biggest publication risk is that secondary-media reporting is being presented as settled primary fact.
